Prompt:
Role: You are an expert Java game developer working on a target game called ApoMario. You are requested to integrate a feature into this game. Follow the task below accurately and verify that the target game works properly with the new feature.

Feature: Highscore is a mechanism for tracking and displaying players performance.
Upon finishing a run/level (clearing a level/losing), the running game itself must record the player's real score, name, and survival time (mm:ss).
The highscore board is displayed in a dedicated panel/view in the game's menu, along with the player's name and is persisted across runs. The names are sorted by points, descending.

Task: Develop the mentioned feature and integrate it into ApoMario by reusing the attached source code of ApoIcarus as much as possible. Attached is also the source code of the most relevant classes in the current version of ApoMario. Wire the feature into the LIVE game so the target game (ApoMario) still compiles and works properly with the new feature.

Context: Use the attached json files, if exist, as relevant context of ApoMario.

Implementation Requirements: For testing purposes, we will assume that the target game includes the following public members (among others): apoMario.game.panels.ApoMarioHighscore:
  - ApoMarioHighscore(java.nio.file.Path store) (store is a file)
  - boolean storeRun(int score, int survivalTime, String playerName)  
  - java.util.List<String> getPlayersNames() 
  - java.util.List<Integer> getPlayersScores()   
  - java.util.List<Integer> getSurvivalTimes()    
  - void persistAcrossRuns()
- public void recordRunEnd(apoMario.level.ApoMarioLevel level) // records the finished run's actual score, player name, and elapsed time as read from the given level object
Your changes ARE NOT limited to these classes and members.

Instructions:
Reuse (first priority):
1. Before writing any new code, inventory the attached Java classes and JSON context and list every existing class, base class, method, field, constant, view/panel, input handler, persistence/IO facility, and utility that could serve the feature. Implement the feature primarily by reusing, extending, composing, and configuring this existing code. DO NOT reuse elements irrelevant to the requested feature or any concrete paths.
2. Introduce a new class or method ONLY when no attached member can be reused or reasonably extended to do the job, and keep such additions minimal. Do not reimplement behavior, data structures, or UI that already exist in the attachments.
3. When new code is unavoidable, model it on the closest existing example in the attachments — same base class, naming conventions, structure, and idioms — so it integrates as an extension of the codebase rather than a parallel implementation.

Develop:
4. Build the feature's core logic and state by reusing the framework patterns already present (base classes, lifecycle/update methods, event and input handling) and the existing helper/utility methods, instead of introducing external libraries or a separate architecture. Do not assume or invent members that are not present in the attachments.
5. If the feature requires persistence, reuse any existing persistence, serialization, or file-IO facility found in the attachments; only if none exists, add file-based persistence with plain Java I/O (load on startup, save when appropriate, start from a clean default state if the file is missing or corrupt).
6. If the feature requires a view/panel/screen, reuse the existing view base class and the existing rendering calls, fonts, colors, buttons, and input handling used by the other views; do not build a new UI stack.
7. If the feature requires user input or configuration, reuse an existing input facility from the attachments if one exists; otherwise use a minimal default consistent with the codebase.
8. Follow the implementation requirements mentioned above.
9. Every contracted method must be fully implemented — a body that is empty or contains only comments where behavior is required is a failure.
10. Every member your delivered code calls on an existing class must either exist in the attachments or be added by a delivered modified version of that class.

Integrate:
11. Apply every integration edit in the delivered code. For each existing class that must change, output the COMPLETE modified class with the changes already applied in place.
12. Wire the feature into the game's real navigation and lifecycle by calling the actual menus, states, screens, update loop, methods, and fields present in the attachments — reuse existing hooks rather than adding new entry points.
13. Trigger the feature at the correct point(s) in the real game flow (the relevant event, lifecycle hook, or end-of-run path), using the game's existing score, timing, and state values instead of recomputing or duplicating them.
14. Keep edits surgical: preserve all unrelated code and existing behavior exactly, and do not refactor or rename existing members.

18. When delivering a modified version of an existing class, reproduce it in FULL — every existing method, constructor, field, and constant with unchanged signatures; classes not shown in the attachments call these members.

Feature: Achievements is a mechanism for tracking three in-game milestones:
POINTS_GOAL is triggered when the player reaches 20,000 points in a single run.
ENEMIES_GOAL is triggered when the player kills at least 3 enemies during the run
TIME_GOAL is triggered when the player survives in one game for more than 60 seconds (60000 ms).
Achievements must unlock from real gameplay by the game itself - the achievement unlocks as soon as its threshold is reached during the run and per-run progress does not carry accross runs.
Unlocked achievements are stored and saved across sessions, so the player's progress is preserved between plays.
The achievements are displayed in a dedicated panel/view in the game's menu.

Role: You are an expert Java game developer working on a target game called ApoBot. You are requested to integrate a feature into this game. Follow the task below accurately and verify that the target game works properly with the new feature.

Feature: Options is a mechanism allowing players to configure two settings of the game: 
Language: the game's menu text is shown in either English or German only.
Sound: whether sound is triggered or not when clicking menu's buttons.
The settings are displayed and modified in a dedicated panel/view in the game's menu.
The chosen preferences are persisted across sessions. Default preferences are English for language and On for sound.
After applying the changes to the game, the selected preferences are applied to the live game - the menu's language matches the chosen language and the menu's buttons sound is ON/OFF, matching the chosen option.
German menu buttons: "Menü", "Spiel"," Tutorial", "Editor".
